svg.text: Word Search II — Trie + DFS for Many Words
Why Word Search II is harder
Naive: for each word in dictionary D, run word-search → O(|D| · m · n · 4 · 3^(L−1)).
For |D| = 10 000 and L = 10, this is hundreds of billions — TLE.
Better: build a trie of D, walk grid once carrying a trie pointer.
Each step descends one char in the trie; if no child, prune entire subtree.
Multiple words share prefixes → one DFS finds all words simultaneously.
Time: O(m · n · 4^Lmax) but pruned heavily by trie structure → milliseconds in practice.
for each cell (r,c): dfs(r, c, root)
ch = grid[r][c]; child = node.children[ch]; if !child: return
if child.word: results.add(child.word); child.word = null # dedupe
mark visited; recurse 4 dirs with child; unmark
if child has no children: prune (delete from parent)
Optimization: aggressive trie pruning shrinks search space as words are found.
